"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The very brief verse notes the LORD speaking to Moses concerning the sin offering. The grammatical voices note the introduction. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying" — the LORD spoke to Moses, now to give the law of the sin offering (the trespass against the commandments through ignorance, vv.2-35); the speaking (God's command, opening the new law of the sin offering); the occasion (after the laws of the burnt, meal, and peace offerings, chs. 1-3, now the offering for sin).

On "the LORD spake unto Moses," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the word. Calvin and Wesley note the LORD speaking (the new law given, the sin offering distinct from the previous offerings, which were sweet savours; the sin offering for the expiation of guilt) — the divine authority (the law from God); the new offering (the sin offering, not a sweet savour but for sin, the first of the expiatory offerings).

On the speaking, the grammatical-historical note the command: the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of the sin offering introduced; the providence (God revealing the way of atonement for sin, the offering for the guilt of transgression). The verse displays the LORD speaking to Moses concerning the sin offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a soul shall sin through ignorance against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things which ought not to be done, and shall do against any of them:" The witnesses note sin through ignorance against God's commandments — the crux of the sinfulness of unwitting sin. On "If a soul shall sin through ignorance against any of the commandments of the LORD," interpreters expound the sin. The dominant reading and the grammatical voices note the ignorance — "if a soul shall sin through ignorance against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things which ought not to be done": the sin offering was for sins committed through ignorance (unwittingly, inadvertently, in error, not presumptuously or with a high hand, Numbers 15:30); the witnesses note the significance — the sins of ignorance (sins committed unwittingly, through inadvertence, weakness, or want of knowledge, yet still sins, still needing atonement); the lesson — the sinfulness of sin even unwitting (sin is sin against God's law, whether known or not, the conscience defiled, the guilt real, the need of atonement; "who can understand his errors? cleanse thou me from secret faults," Psalm 19:12); the holiness of God's law (the smallest, most unwitting breach still a sin requiring atonement); the contrast with presumptuous sin (the high-handed sin, the defiant, for which no sacrifice was appointed, Numbers 15:30-31; Hebrews 10:26); the figure — the need of atonement even for sins of ignorance, fulfilled in Christ who cleanses from all sin, even the secret and unknown (1 John 1:7-9).

On "concerning things which ought not to be done, and shall do against any of them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the transgression. Wesley and Keil note the doing of what ought not to be done (the breach of a negative commandment, the transgression of God's law) — the guilt (the commandment broken, the sin real); the providence (the sin offering for the breach, even unwitting, the atonement provided).

On the sin, the grammatical-historical note the ignorance: sin through ignorance — the unwitting transgression; the providence (the sin of ignorance needing atonement, the holiness of the law, the cleansing in Christ). The verse displays sin through ignorance against God's commandments.

The witnesses agree the substance: the sin offering was for sins committed through ignorance, unwittingly (Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the sinfulness of unwitting sin: "if a soul shall sin through ignorance" — the sins of ignorance, committed unwittingly yet still sins needing atonement (Psalm 19:12), the holiness of God's law (the smallest unwitting breach a real sin), in contrast with presumptuous sin for which no sacrifice was given (Numbers 15:30-31; Hebrews 10:26), the need of atonement even for secret sins, fulfilled in Christ who cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7-9). The verse displays the ignorance crux. The verse marks the sin: through ignorance. The verse displays the law: broken. The verse displays the guilt: real. The verse displays the atonement: needed. The verse displays the sin offering for the priest (vv.3-12).
3:
"If the priest that is anointed do sin according to the sin of the people; then let him bring for his sin, which he hath sinned, a young bullock without blemish unto the LORD for a sin offering." The witnesses note the sin offering for the anointed priest. On "If the priest that is anointed do sin according to the sin of the people," the grammatical voices note the priest. Calvin and Gill note that "if the priest that is anointed do sin according to the sin of the people; then let him bring... a young bullock without blemish unto the LORD for a sin offering" — if the anointed (high) priest sinned, bringing guilt on the people, he was to bring a young bullock (the costliest victim) for a sin offering; the anointed priest (the high priest, the most responsible, his sin the most serious); the sin "according to the sin of the people" (his sin bringing guilt on the people, or as the people would sin, the public consequence of the priest's sin); the bullock (the costliest victim, fitting the gravity of the priest's sin).

On "the priest that is anointed do sin," interpreters expound the gravity. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the priest's sin — the high priest's sin the most grave, bringing guilt on the people; the witnesses note the lesson — the sin of the leader most serious (the higher the office, the greater the sin and its consequence, the priest's sin defiling the sanctuary and the people, the public guilt); the contrast with Christ (the Aaronic high priest himself a sinner, needing to offer for his own sin, Hebrews 5:3; 7:27, "who needeth not daily... to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins"; but Christ the sinless High Priest, needing no offering for himself, holy, harmless, undefiled, Hebrews 7:26-27); the costliest victim (the bullock, the gravity of the sin).

On "a young bullock without blemish unto the LORD for a sin offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the victim. Wesley and Keil note the bullock without blemish (the costliest, unblemished victim, the sin offering) — the figure (the unblemished victim, Christ the lamb without blemish, 1 Peter 1:19); the providence (the priest's sin offering, the figure of the need of atonement and of the sinless Christ). The verse displays the sin offering for the anointed priest.

4:
"And he shall bring the bullock unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD; and shall lay his hand upon the bullock's head, and kill the bullock before the LORD." The witnesses note the laying on of the hand and the killing of the victim — the crux of substitution. On "he shall... lay his hand upon the bullock's head, and kill the bullock before the LORD," interpreters expound the laying on of the hand. The reformed and the dominant reading note the imposition — "he shall... lay his hand upon the bullock's head": the offerer laid his hand on the head of the victim before it was killed; the witnesses note the significance — the laying on of the hand the act of identification and substitution (the offerer putting himself, as it were, on the victim, transferring his sin and guilt to it, the victim standing in his place, bearing his sin and dying in his stead); the lesson — the great principle of substitution (the innocent victim bearing the guilt of the sinner, dying in his place, the sin transferred by the laying on of the hand, the figure of the imputation of our sin to Christ); the figure — Christ the true sin offering, on whom our sin was laid, who bore our sins in his own body (Isaiah 53:6, "the LORD hath laid on him the iniquity of us all"; 1 Peter 2:24, "who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree"; 2 Corinthians 5:21, "he hath made him to be sin for us"); the confession (the laying on of the hand often with confession of sin over the victim, Leviticus 16:21).

On "kill the bullock before the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the death. Wesley and JFB note the killing of the victim (the death of the substitute, the blood shed) — the death (the victim dying in the sinner's place, "without shedding of blood is no remission," Hebrews 9:22); the figure (the death of Christ the substitute for sinners).
